--- SOURCE 3: History of quantum mechanics - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_quantum_mechanics

SUMMARY:
These orbits had fixed or quantized values of angular momentum, a concept proposed by John William Nicholson in a nuclear atom model and adopted by Bohr in his model.: 169 When an atom emitted (or absorbed) energy, the electron did not move in a continuous trajectory from one orbit around the nucleus to another, as might be expected classically. The way that the electrons actually behave is strikingly different from Bohr's atom, and from what we see in the world of our everyday experience; this modern quantum mechanical model of the atom is discussed below. For instance, Heisenberg accepted the theoretical prediction of jumps of electrons between orbitals in an atom, but Schrödinger hoped that a theory based on continuous wave-like properties could avoid what he called (as paraphrased by Wilhelm Wien) "this nonsense about quantum jumps".

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# History of quantum mechanics

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/10_Quantum_Mechanics_Masters.jpg/250px-10_Quantum_Mechanics_Masters.jpg)

The history of [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") is a fundamental part of the [history of modern physics](/wiki/History_of_physics#20th_century:_birth_of_modern_physics "History of physics"). The major chapters of this history begin with the emergence of quantum ideas to explain individual phenomena—blackbody radiation, the photoelectric effect, solar emission spectra—an era called the Old or Older quantum theories.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1) Building on the technology [developed in classical mechanics](/wiki/History_of_classical_mechanics "History of classical mechanics"), the invention of wave mechanics by [Erwin Schrödinger](/wiki/Erwin_Schr%C3%B6dinger "Erwin Schrödinger") and expansion by many others triggers the "modern" era beginning around 1925. [Paul Dirac](/wiki/Paul_Dirac "Paul Dirac")'s relativistic quantum theory work led him to explore quantum theories of radiation, culminating in [quantum electrodynamics](/wiki/Quantum_electrodynamics "Quantum electrodynamics"), the first [quantum field theory](/wiki/Quantum_field_theory "Quantum field theory"). The history of quantum mechanics continues in the [history of quantum field theory](/wiki/History_of_quantum_field_theory "History of quantum field theory"). The history of [quantum chemistry](/wiki/Quantum_chemistry "Quantum chemistry"), theoretical basis of [chemical structure](/wiki/Chemical_structure "Chemical structure"), [reactivity](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction"), and [bonding](/wiki/Chemical_bond "Chemical bond"), interlaces with the events discussed in this article.

The phrase "quantum mechanics" was coined (in German, *Quantenmechanik*) by the group of physicists including [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born"), [Werner Heisenberg](/wiki/Werner_Heisenberg "Werner Heisenberg"), and [Wolfgang Pauli](/wiki/Wolfgang_Pauli "Wolfgang Pauli"), at the [University of Göttingen](/wiki/University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "University of Göttingen") in the early 1920s, and was first used in Born and P. Jordan's September 1925 paper *"Zur Quantenmechanik"*.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The word *[quantum](/wiki/Quantum "Quantum")* comes from the [Latin word](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") for "how much" (as does *quantity*). Something that is *quantized*, as the energy of Planck's harmonic oscillators, can only take specific values. For example, in most countries, money is effectively quantized, with the *quantum of money* being the lowest-value coin in circulation. Mechanics is the branch of science that deals with the action of forces on objects. So, quantum mechanics is the part of mechanics that deals with objects for which particular properties are quantized.

## Triumph and trouble at the end of the classical era

The [discoveries of the 19th century](/wiki/History_of_physics#19th_century "History of physics"), both the successes and failures, set the stage for the emergence of quantum mechanics.

### Wave theory of light

Beginning in 1670 and progressing over three decades, [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") developed and championed his [corpuscular theory](/wiki/Corpuscular_theory "Corpuscular theory"), arguing that the perfectly straight lines of reflection demonstrated light's particle nature, as at that time no wave theory demonstrated travel in straight lines.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1): 19 He explained refraction by positing that particles of light accelerated laterally upon entering a denser medium. Around the same time, Newton's contemporaries [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke") and [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens"), and later [Augustin-Jean Fresnel](/wiki/Augustin-Jean_Fresnel "Augustin-Jean Fresnel"), mathematically refined the wave viewpoint, showing that if light traveled at different speeds in different media, refraction could be easily explained as the medium-dependent propagation of light waves. The resulting [Huygens–Fresnel principle](/wiki/Huygens%E2%80%93Fresnel_principle "Huygens–Fresnel principle") was extremely successful at reproducing light's behaviour and was consistent with [Thomas Young](/wiki/Thomas_Young_(scientist) "Thomas Young (scientist)")'s discovery of [wave interference](/wiki/Wave_interference "Wave interference") of light by his [double-slit experiment](/wiki/Double-slit_experiment "Double-slit experiment") in 1801.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The wave view did not immediately displace the ray and particle view, but began to dominate scientific thinking about light in the mid 19th century, since it could explain polarization phenomena that the alternatives could not.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell") found that he could apply his previously discovered  [equations](/wiki/Maxwell%27s_equations "Maxwell's equations"), along with a slight modification, to describe self-propagating waves of oscillating electric and magnetic fields. It quickly became apparent that visible light, ultraviolet light, and infrared light were all electromagnetic waves of differing frequency.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1): 272 This theory became a critical ingredient in the beginning of quantum mechanics.

### Emerging atomic theory

During the early 19th century, [chemical](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") research by [John Dalton](/wiki/John_Dalton "John Dalton") and [Amedeo Avogadro](/wiki/Amedeo_Avogadro "Amedeo Avogadro") lent weight to the [atomic theory](/wiki/Atomic_theory "Atomic theory") of matter, an idea that [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell"), [Ludwig Boltzmann](/wiki/Ludwig_Boltzmann "Ludwig Boltzmann") and others built upon to establish the [kinetic theory of gases](/wiki/Kinetic_theory_of_gases "Kinetic theory of gases"). The successes of kinetic theory gave further credence to the idea that matter is composed of atoms, yet the theory also had shortcomings that would only be resolved by the development of quantum mechanics.[[7]](#cite_note-Feynman-kinetic-theory-7) The existence of atoms was not universally accepted among physicists or chemists; [Ernst Mach](/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach"), for example, was a staunch anti-atomist.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

![Boltzmann's iodine molecule model](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Boltzmanns-molecule.jpg/250px-Boltzmanns-molecule.jpg)

The earliest hints of problems in classical mechanics were raised in relation to the temperature dependence of the properties of gasses.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
[Ludwig Boltzmann](/wiki/Ludwig_Boltzmann "Ludwig Boltzmann") suggested in 1877 that the energy levels of a physical system, such as a [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule"), could be discrete (rather than continuous). Boltzmann's rationale for the presence of discrete energy levels in molecules such as those of iodine gas had its origins in his [statistical thermodynamics](/wiki/Statistical_thermodynamics "Statistical thermodynamics") and [statistical mechanics](/wiki/Statistical_mechanics "Statistical mechanics") theories and was backed up by [mathematical](/wiki/Mathematical "Mathematical") arguments, as would also be the case twenty years later with the first [quantum theory](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") put forward by [Max Planck](/wiki/Max_Planck "Max Planck").

### Electrons and the nucleus

In the final days of the 1800s, [J. J. Thomson](/wiki/J._J._Thomson "J. J. Thomson") established that [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") carry a negative charge opposite but the same size as that of a hydrogen ion while having a mass over one thousand times less. Many such electrons were known to be associated with every atom.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1): 365 By 1904 Thomson proposed the first atomic model with subatomic constituents, using circulating electrons in a background of positive charge, the so-called [plum pudding model](/wiki/Plum_pudding_model "Plum pudding model").[[10]](#cite_note-Kragh2010-10) Thomson's concepts were supported by early [beta particle](/wiki/Beta_particle "Beta particle") scattering experiments but by 1911 [Hans Geiger](/wiki/Hans_Geiger "Hans Geiger") and his student [Ernest Marsden](/wiki/Ernest_Marsden "Ernest Marsden") demonstrated [backscattering of alpha particles](/wiki/Rutherford_scattering_experiments "Rutherford scattering experiments") which [Ernest Rutherford](/wiki/Ernest_Rutherford "Ernest Rutherford") interpreted as compelling evidence that the positive charge was concentrated in a small volume we now call the [nucleus](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus").[[11]](#cite_note-Heilbron1968-11)

### Radiation theory

![Blackbody radiation curve](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Black_body.svg/250px-Black_body.svg.png)

Throughout the 1800s many studies investigated details in the [spectrum](/wiki/Spectral_line "Spectral line") of intensity versus frequency for light emitted by flames, by the Sun, or red-hot objects.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1): 367 The [Rydberg formula](/wiki/Rydberg_formula "Rydberg formula") effectively summarized the dark lines seen in the spectrum, but Rydberg provided no physical model to explain them. The spectrum emitted by red-hot objects could be explained at high or low wavelengths but the two theories differed.

## Old quantum theory

Quantum mechanics developed in two distinct phases. The first phase, known as the [old quantum theory](/wiki/Old_quantum_theory "Old quantum theory"), began around 1900 with radically new approaches to explanations physical phenomena not understood by classical mechanics of the 1800s.[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1)

### Planck introduces quanta to explain black-body radiation

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Max_Planck_1878.GIF/250px-Max_Planck_1878.GIF)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Hot_metalwork.jpg/500px-Hot_metalwork.jpg)

[Thermal radiation](/wiki/Thermal_radiation "Thermal radiation") is electromagnetic radiation emitted from the surface of an object due to the object's internal energy. If an object is heated sufficiently, it starts to emit light at the red end of the visible [spectrum](/wiki/Spectrum "Spectrum"), as it becomes [red hot](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:red-hot#English "wikt:en:red-hot").

Heating it further causes the color to change from red to yellow, white, and blue, as it emits light at increasingly shorter wavelengths (higher frequencies). A perfect emitter is also a perfect absorber: when it is cold, such an object looks perfectly black, because it absorbs all the light that falls on it and emits none. Consequently, an ideal thermal emitter is known as a [black body](/wiki/Black_body "Black body"), and the radiation it emits is called [black-body radiation](/wiki/Black-body_radiation "Black-body radiation").

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/RWP-comparison.svg/250px-RWP-comparison.svg.png)

By the late 19th century, thermal radiation had been fairly well characterized experimentally. Several formulas had been created that could describe some of the experimental measurements of thermal radiation: how the wavelength at which the radiation is strongest changes with temperature is given by [Wien's displacement law](/wiki/Wien%27s_displacement_law "Wien's displacement law"), the overall power emitted per unit area is given by the [Stefan–Boltzmann law](/wiki/Stefan%E2%80%93Boltzmann_law "Stefan–Boltzmann law"). The best theoretical explanation of the experimental results was the [Rayleigh–Jeans law](/wiki/Rayleigh%E2%80%93Jeans_law "Rayleigh–Jeans law"), which agrees with experimental results well at large wavelengths (or, equivalently, low frequencies), but strongly disagrees at short wavelengths (or high frequencies). In fact, at short wavelengths, classical physics predicted that energy will be emitted by a hot body at an infinite rate. This result, which is clearly wrong, is known as the [ultraviolet catastrophe](/wiki/Ultraviolet_catastrophe "Ultraviolet catastrophe"). However, classical physics led to the Rayleigh–Jeans law, which, as shown in the figure, agrees with experimental results well at low frequencies, but strongly disagrees at high frequencies. Physicists searched for a single theory that explained all the experimental results.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Black_body.svg/500px-Black_body.svg.png)

The first model that was able to explain the full spectrum of thermal radiation was put forward by [Max Planck](/wiki/Max_Planck "Max Planck") in 1900.[[12]](#cite_note-12) He proposed a mathematical model in which the thermal radiation was in equilibrium with a set of [harmonic oscillators](/wiki/Harmonic_oscillator "Harmonic oscillator"). To reproduce the experimental results, he had to assume that each oscillator emitted an integer number of units of energy at its single characteristic frequency, rather than being able to emit any arbitrary amount of energy. In other words, the energy emitted by an oscillator was *quantized*. According to Planck, the quantum of energy E of a light quantum is proportional to its frequency [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") ν. As an equation is it written:

E
=
h
v
.
{\displaystyle E=hv.}
![{\displaystyle E=hv.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/af45c44e1d558909c3a357079e74493144ab6be0)
This is now known as the [Planck relation](/wiki/Planck_relation "Planck relation") and the proportionality constant, *h*, as the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant").

![{\displaystyle E=hv.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/af45c44e1d558909c3a357079e74493144ab6be0)

Planck's law was the first quantum theory in physics, and Planck won the 1918 [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") "in recognition of the services he rendered to the advancement of Physics by his discovery of energy quanta".[[13]](#cite_note-13) At the time, however, Planck's view was that quantization was purely a [heuristic](/wiki/Heuristic "Heuristic") mathematical construct, rather than (as is now believed) a fundamental change in our understanding of the world.[[14]](#cite_note-Kragh-14)

### Einstein applies quanta to explain the photoelectric effect

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Photoelectric_effect_in_a_solid_-_diagram.svg/250px-Photoelectric_effect_in_a_solid_-_diagram.svg.png)

In 1887, [Heinrich Hertz](/wiki/Heinrich_Hertz "Heinrich Hertz") observed that when light with sufficient frequency hits a metallic surface, the surface emits [cathode rays](/wiki/Cathode_rays "Cathode rays").[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1): I:362 Ten years later, J. J. Thomson showed that the many reports of cathode rays were actually "corpuscles" and they quickly came to be called [electrons](/wiki/Electrons "Electrons"). In 1902, [Philipp Lenard](/wiki/Philipp_Lenard "Philipp Lenard") discovered that the maximum possible energy of an ejected electron is unrelated to the [intensity](/wiki/Intensity_(physics) "Intensity (physics)") of the monochromatic light.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This observation is at odds with classical electromagnetism, which predicts that the electron's energy should be proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation.[[16]](#cite_note-Hawking-16): 24

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Einstein_patentoffice.jpg/250px-Einstein_patentoffice.jpg)

In 1905, [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") suggested that even though continuous models of light worked extremely well for time-averaged optical phenomena, for instantaneous transitions the energy in light may occur a finite number of energy quanta.

In the introduction section of his March 1905 paper "On a Heuristic Viewpoint Concerning the Emission and Transformation of Light", Einstein states:

According to the assumption to be contemplated here, when a light ray is spreading from a point, the energy is not distributed continuously over ever-increasing spaces, but consists of a finite number of "energy quanta" that are localized in points in space, move without dividing, and can be absorbed or generated only as a whole.[[17]](#cite_note-Eistein-photoelectric-17)

This has been called the most "revolutionary" sentence written by a twentieth century physicist, meaning that it proposed an idea altered mainstream thinking.[[18]](#cite_note-18): 143
[The energy of a single quantum](/wiki/Photon_energy "Photon energy") of light of frequency 



f
{\displaystyle f}
![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61) is given by the frequency multiplied by the Planck constant 



h
{\displaystyle h}
![{\displaystyle h}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b26be3e694314bc90c3215047e4a2010c6ee184a):

![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61)
![{\displaystyle h}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b26be3e694314bc90c3215047e4a2010c6ee184a)
![{\displaystyle E=hf}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f39fac3593bb1e2dec0282c112c4dff7a99007f6)

Einstein assumed a light quanta transfers all of its energy to a single electron imparting at most an energy *hf* to the electron. Therefore, only the light frequency determines the maximum energy that can be imparted to the electron; the intensity of the photoemission is proportional to the light beam intensity.[[17]](#cite_note-Eistein-photoelectric-17)

Einstein argued that it takes a certain amount of energy, called the *[work function](/wiki/Work_function "Work function")* and denoted by φ, to remove an electron from the metal.[[19]](#cite_note-taylor_127-9-19) This amount of energy is different for each metal. If the energy of the light quanta is less than the work function, then it does not carry sufficient energy to remove the electron from the metal. The threshold frequency, *f*0, is the frequency of a light quanta whose energy is equal to the work function:

![{\displaystyle \varphi =hf_{0}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c24d760b0e2b2331f681340863d6e7b74514fc81)

If *f* is greater than *f*0, the energy *hf* is enough to remove an electron. The ejected electron has a [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy"), *E*k, which is, at most, equal to the light energy minus the energy needed to dislodge the electron from the metal:

![{\displaystyle E_{\text{k}}=hf-\varphi =h(f-f_{0}).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c00ee4a568968d34d86f0a16cac56505712ce4fb)

Einstein's description of light as being composed of energy quanta extended Planck's notion of quantized energy, which is that a single quantum of a given frequency, *f*, delivers an invariant amount of energy, *hf*. Einstein was awarded the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics "for his services to Theoretical Physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect".[[20]](#cite_note-20)
In nature, single quanta are rarely encountered. The Sun and emission sources available in the 19th century emit a vast amount of energy every second. The [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant"), *h*, is so tiny that the amount of energy in each quantum, *hf* is very very small. Light we see includes many trillions of such quanta. [Arthur Compton](/wiki/Arthur_Compton "Arthur Compton")'s demonstration of the  [scattering](/wiki/Compton_scattering "Compton scattering") of light by electrons scattering convinced physicists of the reality of photons.[[21]](#cite_note-Compton1923-21) Compton won the 1927 Nobel Prize in Physics for his discovery.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The term "[photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon")" was introduced in 1926 by [Gilbert N. Lewis](/wiki/Gilbert_N._Lewis "Gilbert N. Lewis").[[23]](#cite_note-www.aps.org-23)

### Quantization of matter: the Bohr model of the atom

[Ernest Rutherford](/wiki/Ernest_Rutherford "Ernest Rutherford")'s discovery of the atomic nucleus in 1911 did not immediately cause atomic models to be revised. Mechanical models with circulating electrons had been proposed for many years but they were known to be unstable.[[11]](#cite_note-Heilbron1968-11)

A second, related puzzle was the [emission spectrum](/wiki/Emission_spectrum "Emission spectrum") of atoms. When a gas is heated, it gives off light only at discrete frequencies. For example, the visible light given off by [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") consists of four different colors, as shown in the picture below. The intensity of the light at different frequencies is also different. By contrast, white light consists of a continuous emission across the whole range of visible frequencies. By the end of the nineteenth century, a simple rule known as [Balmer's formula](/wiki/Balmer_series "Balmer series") showed how the frequencies of the different lines related to each other, though without explaining why this was, or making any prediction about the intensities. The formula also predicted some additional spectral lines in ultraviolet and infrared light that had not been observed at the time. These lines were later observed experimentally, raising confidence in the value of the formula.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Emission_spectrum-H.svg/757px-Emission_spectrum-H.svg.png)

In 1885 the Swiss mathematician [Johann Balmer](/wiki/Johann_Balmer "Johann Balmer") discovered that each wavelength *λ* (lambda) in the visible spectrum of hydrogen is related to some integer *n* by the equation

![{\displaystyle \lambda =B\left({\frac {n^{2}}{n^{2}-4}}\right)\qquad \qquad n=3,4,5,6}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b934f326f434bd8f3742bad1268bfd67236c753a)

where *B* is a constant Balmer determined is equal to 364.56 nm.

In 1888, [Johannes Rydberg](/wiki/Johannes_Rydberg "Johannes Rydberg") generalized and greatly increased the explanatory utility of Balmer's formula. He predicted that *λ* is related to two integers *n* and *m* according to what is now known as the [Rydberg formula](/wiki/Rydberg_formula "Rydberg formula"):[[24]](#cite_note-taylor_147-8-24)

![{\displaystyle {\frac {1}{\lambda }}=R\left({\frac {1}{m^{2}}}-{\frac {1}{n^{2}}}\right),}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b3f75640a81ae9d983b101e3ad86b84709ffbdbd)

where *R* is the [Rydberg constant](/wiki/Rydberg_constant "Rydberg constant"), equal to 0.0110 nm−1, and *n* must be greater than *m*.

The Rydberg formula accounts for the four visible wavelengths of hydrogen by setting *m* = 2 and *n* = 3, 4, 5, 6. It also predicts additional wavelengths in the emission spectrum: for *m* = 1 and for *n* > 1, the emission spectrum should contain certain ultraviolet wavelengths, and for *m* = 3 and *n* > 3, it should also contain certain infrared wavelengths. Experimental observation of these wavelengths came two decades later: in 1908 [Louis Paschen](/wiki/Louis_Paschen "Louis Paschen") found some of the predicted infrared wavelengths, and in 1914 [Theodore Lyman](/wiki/Theodore_Lyman_IV "Theodore Lyman IV") found some of the predicted ultraviolet wavelengths.[[24]](#cite_note-taylor_147-8-24)

Both Balmer's formula and the Rydberg formula involve integers: in modern terms, they imply that some property of the atom is quantized. Understanding exactly what this property was, and why it was quantized, was a major part of the development of quantum mechanics, as shown in the rest of this article.

![Bohr model of hydrogen atom](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Bohr_model_3.jpg/250px-Bohr_model_3.jpg)

In 1913, H. M. Hansen asked [Niels Bohr](/wiki/Niels_Bohr "Niels Bohr") about Balmer's formula. Bohr recalled that "As soon as I saw Balmer's formula, the whole thing was immediately clear to me."[[25]](#cite_note-25) In "On the Constitution of Atoms and Molecules", he proposed  [a new model of the atom](/wiki/Bohr_model "Bohr model") that included quantized electron orbits. In the Bohr model, the hydrogen atom is pictured as a heavy, positively charged nucleus orbited by a light, negatively charged electron. The electron can only exist in certain, discretely separated orbits, labeled by their [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum"), which is restricted to be an integer multiple of the [reduced Planck constant](/wiki/Reduced_Planck_constant "Reduced Planck constant").[[26]](#cite_note-26) The electron moves to a higher or lower orbital by absorbing or emitting a photon of corresponding frequency.

These orbits had fixed or quantized values of angular momentum, a concept proposed by [John William Nicholson](/wiki/John_William_Nicholson "John William Nicholson") in a nuclear atom model and adopted by Bohr in his model.[[27]](#cite_note-McCormmach1966-27): 169 When an atom emitted (or absorbed) energy, the electron did not move in a continuous trajectory from one orbit around the nucleus to another, as might be expected classically. Instead, the electron would jump instantaneously from one orbit to another, giving off the emitted light in the form of a photon.[[28]](#cite_note-WorldBook-28) The possible energies of photons given off by each element were determined by the differences in energy between the orbits, and so the emission spectrum for each element would contain a number of lines.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The model's key success lay in explaining the Rydberg formula for the spectral [emission lines](/wiki/Hydrogen_spectral_series "Hydrogen spectral series") of atomic hydrogen by using the transitions of electrons between orbits.[[30]](#cite_note-Heilbron1969-30): 276 While the Rydberg formula had been known experimentally, it did not gain a theoretical underpinning until the Bohr model was introduced. Not only did the Bohr model explain the reasons for the structure of the Rydberg formula, it also provided a justification for the fundamental physical constants that make up the formula's empirical results.

![Head and shoulders of a young man in a suit and tie](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Niels_Bohr_-_LOC_-_ggbain_-_35303.jpg/250px-Niels_Bohr_-_LOC_-_ggbain_-_35303.jpg)

Starting from only one simple assumption about the rule that the orbits must obey, the Bohr model was able to relate the observed spectral lines in the emission spectrum of hydrogen to previously known constants. In Bohr's model, the electron was not allowed to emit energy continuously and crash into the nucleus: once it was in the closest permitted orbit, it was stable forever. Bohr's model did not explain why the orbits should be quantized in that way, nor was it able to make accurate predictions for atoms with more than one electron, or to explain why some spectral lines are brighter than others.

Some fundamental assumptions of the Bohr model were soon proven wrong—but the key result that the discrete lines in emission spectra are due to some property of the electrons in atoms being quantized is correct. The way that the electrons actually behave is strikingly different from Bohr's atom, and from what we see in the world of our everyday experience; this modern quantum mechanical model of the atom is discussed [below](#Application_to_the_hydrogen_atom).

Bohr theorized that the [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum"), *L*, of an electron is quantized:

![{\displaystyle L=n{\frac {h}{2\pi }}=n\hbar }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a6d24f25ba786b920b9ff7b177ae931a5279c3ee)

where *n* is an integer and *h* and *ħ* are the [Planck constant](/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant") and Planck reduced constant respectively. Starting from this assumption, [Coulomb's law](/wiki/Coulomb%27s_law "Coulomb's law") and the equations of [circular motion](/wiki/Uniform_circular_motion "Uniform circular motion") show that an electron with *n* units of angular momentum orbits a proton at a distance *r* given by

![{\displaystyle r={\frac {n^{2}h^{2}}{4\pi ^{2}k_{e}me^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/64650963e26035c03fda36b9944dcc7205dc9001)

where *k*e is the [Coulomb constant](/wiki/Coulomb_constant "Coulomb constant"), *m* is the mass of an electron, and *e* is the [charge on an electron](/wiki/Elementary_charge "Elementary charge").
For simplicity this is written as

![{\displaystyle r=n^{2}a_{0},\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6457a677b7b76f2ce0fb57f83587c55d91743f56)

where *a*0, called the [Bohr radius](/wiki/Bohr_radius "Bohr radius"), is equal to 0.0529 nm.
The Bohr radius is the radius of the smallest allowed orbit.

The energy of the electron is the sum of its [kinetic](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") and [potential](/wiki/Potential_energy "Potential energy") energies. The electron has kinetic energy by virtue of its actual motion around the nucleus, and potential energy because of its electromagnetic interaction with the nucleus. In the Bohr model this energy can be calculated, and is given by

![{\displaystyle E=-{\frac {k_{\mathrm {e} }e^{2}}{2a_{0}}}{\frac {1}{n^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5a28a06a087400905bb25e5f034fe09731b2ea01)

Thus Bohr's assumption that angular momentum is quantized means that an electron can inhabit only certain orbits around the nucleus and that it can have only certain energies. A consequence of these constraints is that the electron does not crash in

*   ESSAY
*   08 January 2025

How quantum mechanics emerged in a few revolutionary months 100 years ago
=========================================================================

 It began with concerns about the orbits used to explain the motion of electrons in atoms — but quantum theory ended up upending reality itself. 

By
*   [Kristian Camilleri](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-04217-0#author-0)[0](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-04217-0#Aff0)

1.   Kristian Camilleri
    1.   Kristian Camilleri is an associate professor of history and philosophy of science at the University of Melbourne, Australia, and author of _Heisenberg and the Interpretation of Quantum Mechanics_.

[View author publications](https://www.nature.com/search?author=Kristian+Camilleri)

Search author on:[PubMed](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=search&term=Kristian+Camilleri)[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.co.uk/scholar?as_q=&btnG=Search+Scholar&as_sauthors=%22Kristian%2BCamilleri%22)

*   [Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=How+quantum+mechanics+emerged+in+a+few+revolutionary+months+100+years+ago&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fd41586-024-04217-0)
*   [Facebook](http://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fd41586-024-04217-0)
*   [Email](mailto:?subject=How%20quantum%20mechanics%20emerged%20in%20a%20few%20revolutionary%20months%20100%20years%20ago&body=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fd41586-024-04217-0)

![Image 3: Sea against sky during sunset in Heligoland, Germany.](https://media.nature.com/w767/magazine-assets/d41586-024-04217-0/d41586-024-04217-0_50379262.jpg)

The German island of Heligoland, where Werner Heisenberg wrote his breakthrough paper on quantum theory. Credit: Getty

[Access through your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fd41586-024-04217-0)

[Buy or subscribe](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-04217-0#access-options)

In July 1925, a 23-year-old German physicist submitted a paper[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-024-04217-0#ref-CR1) to the journal _Zeitschrift für Physik_ entitled ‘On quantum-theoretical reinterpretation of kinematic and mechanical relationships’. The publication of Werner Heisenberg’s article was arguably the moment that ushered in the modern age of quantum mechanics, thus setting in train an astonishing revolution in our basic understanding of physics that has repercussions to this day. The United Nations has proclaimed 2025 to be the International Year of Quantum Science and Technology, in no small measure because of the events that began to unfold at breathtaking speed 100 years ago.

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://energywavetheory.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/classical-vs-quantum-mechanics.png
DESCRIPTION: A diagram illustrating the distinction between classical mechanics and quantum mechanics, emphasizing their differences in scale, with a depiction of a planet representing classical mechanics at larger sizes and tiny particles representing quantum mechanics at smaller scales.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://physicsgirl.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Comparison-between-Classical-and-Quantum-Mechanics.png
DESCRIPTION: A stick figure with a dog on a slope labeled "Classical" is contrasted with a staircase labeled "Quantum" featuring smiling faces, suggesting a playful comparison between classical and quantum physics concepts.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/d/images/thumb/6/64/Physicsdomains.jpg/380px-Physicsdomains.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A four-quadrant diagram illustrating the transition from classical mechanics to quantum mechanics and relativistic mechanics, with axes representing size scales and speed, and including labels for quantum mechanics and quantum field theory.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://0.academia-photos.com/attachment_thumbnails/118643506/mini_magick20241004-1-f0m7tv.png?1728038626
DESCRIPTION: The text discusses the historical development of quantum mechanics, highlighting key contributions from physicists like Max Born, Werner Heisenberg, Wolfgang Pauli, Isaac Newton, Robert Hooke, Christian Huygens, and Thomas Young, and covers topics such as wave theory of light, quantization, and the transition from classical to modern physics.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://slideplayer.com/slide/13036399/79/images/2/Quantum+vs.+Classical+World.jpg
DESCRIPTION: None

enriched_points.point_title: From Classical Mechanics to Quantum Mechanics
enriched_points.expanded_content: The transition from classical to quantum mechanics marked a paradigm shift, highlighting the limitations of classical physics in explaining atomic and subatomic phenomena.
Relativity revolutionized our understanding of time and space, introducing concepts like time dilation and the interdependence of mass and energy, fundamentally altering physics.
Statistical mechanics bridges microscopic particle behavior with macroscopic phenomena, enabling the prediction of thermodynamic properties and enhancing our understanding of complex systems.
